skill.name: memory-forensics
skill.description: Guide memory acquisition, Volatility analysis, artifact extraction, and defensive malware investigation from RAM captures.
skill.summary: Analyze memory dumps with structured forensic workflows and Volatility guidance.
skill.author: sickn33

security_audit.summary: The static external-command findings are mostly markdown examples for forensic tools, not hidden code execution. However, the skill gives dual-use guidance for privileged live memory acquisition and credential extraction from memory, so it should not be published without additional review and safeguards.

high_findings.title: Privileged Live Memory Acquisition Commands
high_findings.description: The skill includes examples that load a kernel module, read /dev/mem, copy /proc/kcore, and run osxpmem with sudo. These are legitimate forensic techniques, but they require elevated access and can expose full system memory or affect a live host if misused.
high_findings.confidence_reasoning: The commands are explicit and require privileged access to live memory sources. The surrounding context is forensic acquisition, so the risk is dual-use rather than confirmed malicious.

high_findings.title: Credential Extraction From Memory
high_findings.description: The skill documents hashdump, lsadump, cachedump, and Mimikatz-style credential extraction from memory. These commands can support incident response, but they can also enable credential theft if used without authorization.
high_findings.confidence_reasoning: The section is clearly about extracting hashes, LSA secrets, and cached credentials. The document frames this as forensic analysis, but the misuse potential is direct and high.

medium_findings.title: Process Memory and File Dumping Workflows
medium_findings.description: Several examples dump process memory, suspicious executables, registry hives, and files from captured memory. This is normal memory forensics behavior, but dumped artifacts can contain secrets, private data, or malware samples.
medium_findings.confidence_reasoning: The commands directly dump memory and artifacts. The risk is credible, but the examples are standard defensive forensic procedures.

medium_findings.title: Offensive Tooling References Used for Detection
medium_findings.description: The skill references process injection, rootkit behavior, shellcode patterns, and a Cobalt Strike YARA rule. These references are presented as detection content, so the Cobalt Strike static blocker appears to be contextual rather than malicious.
medium_findings.confidence_reasoning: The static keywords are present, but the surrounding text consistently describes detection and analysis. I did not find evidence of payload generation, deployment, or evasion instructions.

medium_findings.title: Network and System Reconnaissance of Memory Images
medium_findings.description: The skill includes commands to enumerate processes, network connections, modules, mounts, environment variables, and command lines from memory images. These are expected forensic tasks, but they can reveal sensitive host and user data.
medium_findings.confidence_reasoning: The commands clearly enumerate sensitive state from memory. They operate on memory captures rather than scanning third-party systems, which lowers the severity.

low_findings.title: Hardcoded URL Is Documentation
low_findings.description: The hardcoded URL points to the Volatility symbol download location in a comment. It is not used for data exfiltration or automatic network access.
low_findings.confidence_reasoning: The URL appears only in an installation note. No command sends local data to that URL.

low_findings.title: Markdown Code Fences Flagged as Ruby Backticks
low_findings.description: Many static external-command findings come from markdown code fences such as powershell, bash, c, python, and yara. These are examples in documentation, not executable Ruby backtick calls in code.
low_findings.confidence_reasoning: The flagged characters delimit markdown examples. There is no Ruby source file or executable wrapper in the skill.

dangerous_patterns.title: Root Access to Live Memory Devices
dangerous_patterns.description: Examples access live memory sources with sudo and device or proc files. This pattern can expose credentials, private data, and kernel memory.
dangerous_patterns.confidence_reasoning: The commands explicitly use sudo with kernel memory acquisition mechanisms. Their forensic purpose is clear, but the operational risk is high.

dangerous_patterns.title: Credential Dump Plugin Usage
dangerous_patterns.description: The skill lists memory plugins for password hashes, LSA secrets, and cached domain credentials. This is sensitive functionality even in a defensive workflow.
dangerous_patterns.confidence_reasoning: The listed plugin names and comments directly identify credential material. The section does not include authorization checks or handling warnings.

dangerous_patterns.title: Bulk Extraction of Memory Strings and Artifacts
dangerous_patterns.description: The skill shows bulk string extraction and pattern matching across memory captures. This can reveal URLs, IP addresses, tokens, and user data.
dangerous_patterns.confidence_reasoning: The commands intentionally extract large amounts of text from memory. The risk depends on authorization and handling of the resulting files.

content.user_title: Investigate Memory Dumps with Forensic Workflows
content.value_statement: Memory investigations are hard to structure during incidents. This skill gives repeatable acquisition, Volatility, artifact extraction, and analysis workflows for authorized forensic work.
actual_capabilities: Guides RAM acquisition options for Windows, Linux, macOS, and virtual machines.
Explains Volatility 3 setup, symbols, and common plugin families.
Provides workflows for process, network, registry, file, and timeline analysis.
Describes indicators for injection, rootkits, suspicious VAD regions, and hidden processes.
Shows how to use YARA, strings, and FLOSS with memory captures.
Lists forensic best practices for integrity, documentation, and chain of custody.
limitations: It does not include tools, binaries, plugins, or automated acquisition scripts.
It requires legal authorization before live memory collection or credential analysis.
Several commands require administrator or root access and can affect live systems.
Results depend on dump quality, operating system symbols, and tool compatibility.

use_cases.title: Triage a Suspected Compromise
use_cases.description: Create a process, network, and command-line review plan for a captured memory image.

use_cases.title: Analyze Malware Behavior in RAM
use_cases.description: Use memory plugins, strings, and YARA scanning to identify injected code and suspicious artifacts.

use_cases.title: Build a Forensic Acquisition Checklist
use_cases.description: Prepare documented acquisition steps that preserve integrity and chain of custody.

prompt_templates.title: Plan Basic Memory Triage
prompt_templates.scenario: Beginner triage of a memory image
prompt_templates.prompt: I have an authorized memory image from a Windows host. Create a first-pass triage checklist using Volatility 3, with expected outputs and review priorities.

prompt_templates.title: Compare Process Views
prompt_templates.prompt: Help me compare normal process listings with scan-based results from a memory image. Explain what mismatches could mean and how to validate them.

prompt_templates.title: Investigate Suspicious Injection
prompt_templates.prompt: Design a workflow to investigate suspected process injection in a memory dump. Include VAD review, suspicious region dumping, strings, and YARA validation.

prompt_templates.title: Correlate Full Incident Evidence
prompt_templates.prompt: Create a memory-forensics investigation plan that correlates process, network, registry, timeline, and file artifacts with disk and network evidence.

output_examples.input: Plan a first-pass review of a Windows memory dump.
output: Start with image metadata and symbol validation.
Review process lists, parent-child relationships, and command lines.
Check network connections and suspicious services.

output_examples.input: Explain how to assess a suspicious process from memory.
output_examples.output: The response would compare process metadata, command line, loaded modules, handles, memory protections, and related network activity. It would recommend artifact dumping only after documenting authorization and evidence handling.

output_examples.input: Create an acquisition checklist for a live Linux host.
output: Record host time, tool version, operator, and target details.
Use the least intrusive approved acquisition method.
Hash the capture and preserve chain-of-custody records.

best_practices: Confirm authorization, scope, and evidence handling rules before acquisition or credential review.
Hash captures immediately and record tool versions, timestamps, operators, and commands used.
Correlate memory findings with disk, endpoint, and network evidence before making conclusions.
anti_patterns: Do not run live acquisition commands on systems outside the approved investigation scope.
Do not treat one plugin result as conclusive without cross-checking with another source.
Do not store memory dumps, strings, or credential artifacts in unsecured locations.

faq.question: Does this skill perform memory acquisition automatically?
faq.answer: No. It provides guidance and example workflows. Users must supply tools, permissions, and evidence handling controls.

faq.question: Which tool does the skill focus on?
faq.answer: It focuses on Volatility 3 and related forensic utilities for strings, YARA scanning, and obfuscated string extraction.

faq.question: Can it analyze Windows, Linux, and macOS memory?
faq.answer: Yes. It includes examples for all three platforms, but plugin support depends on image type and tool compatibility.

faq.question: Is this appropriate for production incident response?
faq.answer: It can support authorized incident response, but teams should follow their legal, privacy, and evidence handling procedures.

faq.question: Why is the risk level high?
faq.answer: The content includes privileged memory acquisition and credential extraction guidance. These techniques are sensitive even when used defensively.

faq.question: Does it include malware or exploit code?
faq.answer: No evidence found of malware deployment or exploit code. It references malware techniques for detection and analysis.
